query: AHI Index for mild sleep apnea

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The AASM recommends using the term respiratory event index (REI) to differentiate the indices of respiratory events generated by a home sleep study (without recorded sleep). The AHI and REI represent the average number of obstructive events per hour, during sleep or recording time, respectively. Although most portable monitoring devices include flow sensors, other technologies, such as peripheral arterial tonometry (PAT), use alternative methods without flow to identify sleep-disordered breathing events. The severity of OSA obtained using PAT devices is referred to as pAHI, which has been reported to provide indices similar to those derived from PSG-based AHI. [42] The severity of OSA in adults is classified based on AHI, REI, or pAHI as follows: Mild: 5 to 15 events per hour Moderate: Greater than 15 to 30 events per hour Severe: Greater than 30 events per hour

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Apnea Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An understanding of key indexes of sleep-disordered breathing is required to understand the data of a polysomnogram and diagnose obstructive sleep apnea. The Apnea Index (AI) refers to the total number of apneas per hour of sleep. The Hypopnea Index (HI) is the total number of apneas per hour of sleep. The AI and HI are used to calculate the Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI), which is the total apneas or hypopneas per hour of sleep. This calculation serves as one of the important determinants in the diagnosis of OSA. The RERA Index refers to the total number of RERAs that occur per hour of sleep. The Respiratory Disturbance Index (RDI) is an index that is calculated as the total number of apneas, hypopneas, and RERAs per hour of sleep. The Central Apnea Intex is another useful index calculated by the total number of central apneas per hour of sleep. Similarly, the Mixed Apnea Index may be calculated to find the total number of mixed apneas per hour of sleep.

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with a high pretest probability of moderate-to-severe OSA who receive negative or technically inadequate portable monitoring results should undergo in-laboratory PSG.

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Apnea Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of OSA is confirmed on polysomnogram if there are five or more predominantly obstructive respiratory events per hour (which refers to an AHI or RDI 5 or greater) in a patient who has one or more sleep-associated symptoms or conditions (which include sleepiness, fatigue, sleep that is unrestful, insomnia, apneas, gasping or choking, consistent snoring breathing interruptions witnessed by a bed partner, mood disorder, diagnosed hypertension, cognitive dysfunction, cerebrovascular accident, coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation, congestive heart disease, or diabetes mellitus type II). Alternatively, the diagnosis of OSA is also confirmed if there are fifteen or more predominantly obstructive respiratory events per hour (referring to an AHI or RDI of 15 or greater), regardless of associated symptoms or conditions. OSA is generally classified as mild, moderate, or severe. Mild OSA refers to an AHI or RDI between 5 and 14 on a polysomnogram. Moderate OSA refers to an AHI or RDI between 15 and 30. Patients with an AHI or RDI greater than 30 on a polysomnogram are categorized as having severe OSA. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Moderate and severe sleep apnea can be detected using these tests. However, due to the potential underestimation of the apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) relative to the total recording time (which may exceed the total sleep time measured in an in-laboratory study), mild sleep apnea may go undiagnosed. In such cases, a repeat in-laboratory study may be necessary. A proposed algorithm for the appropriate use of portable monitoring and in-laboratory PSG is outlined in the Image. Sleep Apnea Testing Modalities.

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep and Neurodegenerative Disorders -- Treatment / Management -- Obstructive Sleep Apnea
retrieved_docs.content: Patient education and behavior modification, such as controlling obesity, avoiding alcohol, and sedating medications, are recommended. An apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) >15 events/hour warrants positive airway pressure (PAP) therapy. [79] PAP therapy is also recommended for patients with mild OSA (AHI of 5 to 15) who have excessive daytime sleepiness. [80]

retrieved_docs.title: Mallampati Score -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A correlation has also been established between the Mallampati score and OSA, the most prevalent sleep-related breathing disorder. OSA results from upper airway collapse during sleep due to decreased muscle tone. The Mallampati score has been identified as an independent predictor of both the presence and severity of OSA. The odds of having OSA double for every 1-point increase in the Mallampati class, and the apnea-hypopnea index rises by more than 5 events per hour on average. [22] As such, Mallampati assessment may be useful in outpatient clinical settings to help identify patients at risk for OSA prior to polysomnographic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: During PSG, certain specific parameters of sleep quality and quantity are measured. This is done by using sensors to measure brain activity, heart rate, nasal and oral air movement, blood oxygen levels, limb movements, eye movements, and the presence of snoring. The data collected allows for calculations to include sleep onset latency, sleep efficiency, and time in each stage of sleep. Important for the diagnosis of OSA, a PSG will measure the apnea/hypopnea index (AHI), which is the average of the apneic and hypopneic episodes per hour of sleep. An AHI score of 1 to 4.9 events/hour is mild OSA, 5 to 9.9 events/hour is moderate, and more than 9 events/hour is severe. [3] An AHI of 1 or greater is abnormal in children up to 13 years old, although there is some controversy over the clinical significance of an AHI 1 to 1.9 events/hour. [1] From 13 to 17 years of age, either pediatric or adult criteria can be used depending on what is most appropriate based on the clinical picture and patient development. Because children have higher airway integrity and less risk of collapse like adults, their PSG is usually dominated by hypopneic events rather than frank apnea. Interestingly, in the diagnosis of pediatric OSA, hypercarbia may also be present in the pediatric population that is absent in the adult OSA population. This is caused by a state of prolonged hypoventilation and is less common in adults. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Airway Obstruction -- Evaluation -- Sleep Study
retrieved_docs.content: A sleep study or polysomnography should be performed to assess OSA formally. The important results of a sleep study are the patient’s oxygen nadir and the (AHI). The AHI averages the number of apneic and hypopneic episodes per hour. An AHI of anything above 1 is abnormal in a child, and there is a severity grading system for increasing numbers of AHI in adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A board-certified sleep specialist, or an individual meeting the eligibility criteria for the sleep medicine certification examination, must review the raw data from portable monitoring using scoring criteria that align with current AASM standards. [39] Under these specified conditions, portable monitoring may be used for unattended studies in the patient's home.

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A follow-up visit should be scheduled to review test results for all patients undergoing portable monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The portable monitoring device must display raw data and allow a trained sleep technologist or technician to manually score or edit automated scoring.

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The disease burden in mild OSA is controversial and is primarily based on associated clinical sequelae, such as excessive daytime sleepiness, sleep maintenance insomnia, and cognitive dysfunction. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Recent studies have challenged the traditional definition and scoring criteria of OSA in adults due to its limitations in capturing the pathophysiological impact on individual patients. [19] Various metrics have been proposed to improve the precision in diagnosing OSA, [44] including hypoxic burden, nocturnal heart rate changes, total sleep time with SpO 2 less than 90% (TST90), duration of obstructive events, sleep arousal burden, and even genetic factors. [45] [46] [47] [48] [49] [50] [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep and Neurodegenerative Disorders -- Evaluation -- Obstructive sleep apnea
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic evaluation should be performed on patients with excessive daytime sleepiness, nighttime snoring, or complications associated with sleep apnea. Some of the standard evaluation tools include the STOP-Bang questionnaire and the Epworth Sleepiness Scale. In-laboratory PSG is considered the gold standard test. An HSAT could be considered in patients with a high pretest probability of moderate to severe sleep apnea, suggested by daytime hypersomnolence, habitual snoring, witnessed apnea, gasping, or choking during sleep. [62]

retrieved_docs.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The STOP-BANG questionnaire can be used to assess the probability of moderate-to-severe OSA. A high risk is indicated if "YES" is selected for 5 or more items, while a low risk is indicated if "YES" is answered for fewer than 3 items.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: Apnea (الاِختِنَاق) is the overarching respiratory disturbance that encompasses the broader category of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ). Within this category, the clinical condition described as respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) represents a more severe manifestation. Thus, adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest are subtypes of the respiratory disorder, which itself is nested under the primary concept of apnea, illustrating a hierarchical relationship that links a fundamental breathing abnormality to increasingly critical respiratory pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2879
retrieved_docs.content: Apnea (الاِختِنَاق) is a specific form of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) and is directly linked to low oxygen levels in the blood (نقص الأكسجين في الدم), indicating that this breathing interruption can result in hypoxemia.

retrieved_docs.content: الدماغي غير المعدي, SMQ) and is grouped with broader cognitive impairments such as attention deficit, reduced concentration, and excessive early morning sleep, underscoring these features as characteristic of this syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. The non‑infectious delirium/brain disorder (SMQ) similarly features apathy (اللامبالاة), attention impairment, reduced concentration, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. Consequently, excessive daytime sleepiness serves as a linking symptom across arousal disturbances in both encephalopathic and delirium‑like SMQ categories, underscoring its clinical relevance for clinicians managing non‑infectious brain disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1701
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is a specific adult respiratory condition that is classified under the broader category of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1162
retrieved_docs.content: The syndrome “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)” is characterized as a collection of related neuropsychiatric manifestations. It includes “اضطراب التصرف” as one of its constituent features, and “اللامبالاة” is also identified as a member of this syndrome. In addition, the SMQ encompasses deficits in attention (“خلل في الانتباه”), reduced ability to maintain focus (“ضعف التركيز”), and an early presentation of excessive daytime sleepiness (“فرط النوم في النهار الأولي”). Together, these elements define the clinical profile of “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)”, indicating that patients with this condition may exhibit behavior disorder, apathy, attention and concentration problems, and initial excessive daytime sleepiness as interrelated components of the disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1691
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is recognized as both the specific medical procedure and the category that defines it, effectively classifying itself within the domain of airway management and ventilatory support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: ضيق النَّفَس is recognized as having a relationship with مرض نفسي جسدي, indicating that episodes of shortness of breath can be associated with psychosomatic or psychophysiological disorders, where psychological factors manifest as physical respiratory symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: transient and persistent sleep‑onset disorders are considered manifestations of non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ), which also presents with motor hyperactivity, muscular hyperactivity, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. Apathy belongs to the delirium/non‑infectious brain pathology (SMQ) category and is linked to attention deficits, poor concentration, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. The overlapping symptomatology of hyperactivity, excessive daytime sleepiness, and concentration impairment across depression, non‑infectious encephalitis, and delirium underscores the necessity for careful differential diagnosis and integrated, individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 921
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is identified and interpreted through increased blood pressure measurements, which are central to its clinical assessment. Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis exhibits clinical features that are similar to those of essential hypertension, highlighting overlapping manifestations and the need to consider both conditions when evaluating patients with elevated blood pressure and related renal changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition that reflects an overall increase in arterial pressure. Within the broader vascular category, several specific hypertensive disorders are recognized as subtypes, including أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). Although these entities share the common feature of elevated blood pressure, they differ in their underlying mechanisms, affected vascular territories, and clinical manifestations. Recognizing that elevated mean arterial pressure and its vascular subtypes are interrelated helps clinicians accurately diagnose, monitor, and tailor treatment strategies for patients presenting with various forms of hypertension, thereby improving patient care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1176
retrieved_docs.content: Collectively, these interconnected concepts illustrate the hierarchical, methodological and risk‑related links among bronchoscopy, nuclear medicine, airway management, respiratory therapy, various catheterization and intubation techniques, and their relevance to comprehensive patient assessment, monitoring and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are both classified as vascular abnormalities, reflecting increased pressure within the circulatory system. The broader vascular category also encompasses severe pressure disorders such as أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate a spectrum of vascular pressure elevations that are important for clinicians to recognize, as they can signal acute emergencies or chronic pulmonary vascular disease and guide appropriate monitoring and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure are all classified as Vascular conditions, indicating that each represents a form of increased arterial pressure within the vascular system. The Vascular category also encompasses أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate the spectrum of vascular hypertension, ranging from chronic elevations in diastolic, systolic, and mean arterial pressures to acute severe hypertension and pulmonary‑specific hypertension, and recognizing them as children of the Vascular classification aids clinicians in organizing assessment and treatment strategies for hypertension‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1717
retrieved_docs.content: الاِختِنَاق is the Arabic term that maps to نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating a physiological state characterized by insufficient oxygen in the blood. This hypoxic condition can compromise cellular metabolism and is clinically important in the assessment and management of patients with respiratory, circulatory, or metabolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2292
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, also described as anxiety disorder due to a known physiological condition, is a distinct psychiatric diagnosis that requires the presence of specific diagnostic criteria. This disorder is characterized by anxiety symptoms that are directly attributable to an underlying medical or physiological condition, and clinicians must identify the causal medical condition and apply the established criteria to confirm the diagnosis.
